coredata.dc:title: Improved photocatalytic activity of few-layer Bi4O5I2 nanosheets induced by efficient charge separation and lower valence position
coredata.dc:creator: Xia, Jiexiang
Ji, Mengxia
Di, Jun
Wang, Bin
Yin, Sheng
He, Minqiang
Zhang, Qi
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasmall few-layer Bi4O5I2 nanosheets were controlled prepared via an ionic liquid [Hmim]I (1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide)-mannitol assisted solvothermal method accompanied by adjusting to the proper pH. Multiple characterizations were employed to investigate the composition, structure, morphology, optical absorption property and photocatalytic performance of the ultrasmall few-layer Bi4O5I2 nanosheets materials. During the synthesis process, ionic liquid acted as solvent, capping agent and provided the I source to form the Bi4O5I2 nanostructure, resulting in the thinnest Bi4O5I2 materials (about 6 layers) reported up to now. DLS analysis indicated the Bi4O5I2 diameter was mainly distributed in the range of 37–52 nm. Organic dye rhodamine B (RhB) and colourless ciprofloxacin (CIP) were used as target pollutants to estimate the photocatalytic performance of as-prepared Bi4O5I2 nanosheets. PL analysis showed that Bi4O5I2 owned the lower recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carriers. The improved photocatalytic activity of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets was attributed to the ultrasmall few-layer structure and varied energy band structure which favored the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs and the lower valence position of Bi4O5I2 greatly increased the oxidizing ability. Different from the active species of BiOI, the photogenerated holes and ·OH were determined to be the main active species of Bi4O5I2 during the photodegradation process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi4O5I2
Ionic liquid
Photocatalysis
Visible light

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of as-synthesized ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets and pure BiOI nanosheets.
XPS spectra of the as-prepared ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets. (a) Survey of the sample; (b) Bi 4f; (c) O 1s; (d) I 3d.
(a) SEM image, (b) and (c) TEM images of the as-synthesized ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets; (d) SEM image BiOI nanosheets.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets photocatalysts and BiOI nanosheets.
Nitrogen absorption-desorption isotherms of ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets.
Raman spectra of BiOI nanosheets and ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets photocatalysts.
(a) Photocurrent response and (b) electrochemical impedance spectrum of BiOI nanosheets and ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets.

(a) The absorption ability of ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets in the RhB solution without visible light irradiation; (b) photocatalytic degradation of RhB over TiO2, C3N4, BiOI nanosheets and ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets and (c) kinetic curves for the degradation of RhB over ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets.
Photocatalytic degradation of CIP over C3N4, BiOI nanosheets and ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets.
Cycling runs for the photodegradation of RhB in the presence of ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets under visible irradiation.
XRD patterns of the ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets materials before and after the cycling photocatalytic experiments.
Free radicals trapping experiment of ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets under visible light irradiation.
ESR spectra of radical adducts trapped by DMPO in Bi4O5I2 samples (a) methanol dispersion and (b) aqueous dispersion under visible light irradiation.
Illumination of estimated band positions and redox potential for BiOI and ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanomaterials.

head.ce:title: Improved photocatalytic activity of few-layer Bi4O5I2 nanosheets induced by efficient charge separation and lower valence position

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hainan Provincial Key Lab of Fine Chemistry, HainanUniversity, Haikou 570228, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hainan Provincial Key Lab of Fine Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasmall few-layer Bi4O5I2 nanosheets were controlled prepared via an ionic liquid [Hmim]I (1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide)-mannitol assisted solvothermal method accompanied by adjusting to the proper pH. Multiple characterizations were employed to investigate the composition, structure, morphology, optical absorption property and photocatalytic performance of the ultrasmall few-layer Bi4O5I2 nanosheets materials. During the synthesis process, ionic liquid acted as solvent, capping agent and provided the I source to form the Bi4O5I2 nanostructure, resulting in the thinnest Bi4O5I2 materials (about 6 layers) reported up to now. DLS analysis indicated the Bi4O5I2 diameter was mainly distributed in the range of 37–52 nm. Organic dye rhodamine B (RhB) and colourless ciprofloxacin (CIP) were used as target pollutants to estimate the photocatalytic performance of as-prepared Bi4O5I2 nanosheets. PL analysis showed that Bi4O5I2 owned the lower recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carriers. The improved photocatalytic activity of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets was attributed to the ultrasmall few-layer structure and varied energy band structure which favored the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs and the lower valence position of Bi4O5I2 greatly increased the oxidizing ability. Different from the active species of BiOI, the photogenerated holes and ·OH were determined to be the main active species of Bi4O5I2 during the photodegradation process.

Ultrasmall few-layer Bi4O5I2 nanosheets were controlled prepared by using an ionic liquid [Hmim]I (1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide)-mannitol assisted solvothermal method accompanied by adjusting to the pr oper pH. During the synthesis process, ionic liquid acted as solvent, capping agent and provided the I source to form the Bi4O5I2 nanostructure, resulting in the thinnest Bi4O5I2 materials (about 6 layers) reported up to now. Organic dye rhodamine B (RhB) and colourless ciprofloxacin (CIP) were used as target pollutants to estimate the photocatalytic performance of as-prepared Bi4O5I2 nanosheets. The improved photocatalytic activity of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets was attributed to the ultrasmall few-layer structure and varied energy band structure which favored the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs and the lower valence position of Bi4O5I2 greatly increased the oxidizing ability. Different from the active species of BiOI, the photogenerated holes and ·OH were determined to be the main active species of Bi4O5I2 during the photodegradation process.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Along with economic and social development, environmental pollution has seriously affected human beings. For the last few years, many studies found that photocatalysts show excellent abilities in solar energy transformation and environment governance. As we all known, semiconductors have been intensively investigated as photocatalysts for the pollutants removal and water splitting due to their environment-friendly and low-cost superiority [1]. However, the wide band gap materials such as TiO2, ZnO and BiPO4 can only perform photoresponse under UV irradiation [2,3]. In order to take full advantage of sunlight, many visible light responsed photocatalysts have been developed [4–7].

Bismuth oxyhalide, BiOX (X = Cl, Br, and I) as a group of promising photocatalysts, possessing the stable structure and suitable band gap, has aroused much attentions for the excellent photocatalytic properties [8–15]. The special layered structure by interlacing [Bi2O2] slabs with double halogen slabs endows the BiOX with self-built internal static electric fields that results in the excellent photocatalytic activity [16,17]. Among the BiOX, BiOI owns a broad visible light absorption region and the smallest bandgap, thus attracts numerous attentions [18–20]. It has been reported that BiOI materials with various nanostructure such as nanosheets, nanoflowers and hollow microspheres have been prepared and used for efficient degradation of organic pollutants [21–23]. What's more, a series of strategies have been explored to further improve the photocatalytic activity of BiOI. For instance, the crystal face regulation [24], surface functionalization (MWCNT [25], superficial oxygen vacancy [26]), and building the heterojunction such as g-C3N4/BiOI [27], AgI/BiOI [28], ZnWO4/BiOI [29], BiPO4/BiOI [30] and BiOI/Bi2MoO6 [31]. However, the high valence band (VB) position of BiOI limits the oxidizability and goes against the decomposition of pollutants. And the rapidly recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs is another drawback to limit the photocatalytic activity of BiOI.

Very recently, the bismuth-rich and nanoscaling strategies have been certified to be the effective strategies to improve the photocatalytic performance of bismuth oxyhalides. Up to now, some bismuth-rich bismuth oxyiodide such as Bi5O7I, Bi7O9I3 and Bi4O5I2 have been synthesized [32–35]. However, the enhancement mechanism of activity has not been explored clearly. And these bismuth-rich bismuth oxyiodide materials were large size and no ultrasmall nanostructure was controlled prepared up to now. The charge carriers generated deeply within the semiconductor would take a longer time to reach the surface when compared with these generated close to the surface. Thus, constructing the nanoscale bismuth-rich bismuth oxyiodide with ultrasmall size and few layer ultrathin structure could endow strikingly faster transfer rate of carrier from internal to the surface and result in the effective separation of electron-hole pairs.

The application of ionic liquids (ILs) for the preparation of inorganic nanomaterial has aroused increasing attention in recent years, due to the excellent physical and chemical properties such as thermostability, dissolubility, lower toxicity and tractability [36]. Several small-size and ultrathin nanomaterials have been controllable prepared by using ILs as capping agent [36–39]. During our previous exploration, the Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets have been prepared under H2O-ethylene glycol system by our group [40]. The IL acted as crucial role during the preparation process. However, the size of Bi4O5Br2 was not uniform and the VB position was still too high (about 0.94 eV). The mannitol solution has been found to be beneficial for the formation of ultrathin two-dimensional nanosheets. Therefore, it was desirable to explore the synthesis of ultrasmall few-layer Bi4O5I2 nanomaterials under ILs-mannitol systems.

In this work, ILs combining mannitol-assisted solvothermal method has been employed to prepare Bi4O5I2 materials accompanied by facile pH regulation. During the reaction process, the ionic liquid [Hmim]I acted as solvent, capping agent and I source at the same time. And the mannitol worked as the surfactant to combine with ionic liquid to further control the ultrasmall few-layer structure of Bi4O5I2. The composition, structure and morphology of the as-prepared Bi4O5I2 materials were explored by various characterizations. The photocatalytic activity of the Bi4O5I2 materials was evaluated by using rhodamine B (RhB) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) as target pollutants. The main active species were also determined.
All of the chemicals are analytically pure and used as received. The ionic liquid 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([Hmim]I) (99%) was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co. Ltd.

The Bi4O5I2 nanosheets were synthesized via a facile solvothermal route using Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and [Hmim]I. In a typical synthesis, 1 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 1 mmol [Hmim]I were dissolved into 10 mL 0.1 mol/L mannitol solution with continuous stirring at room temperature, respectively. Then the latter mannitol solution dissolved [Hmim]I was added to the former by drops under magnetic stirring. After that, the pH of the suspension was adjusted to 12 using 1 mol L−1 NaOH and further stirring for 30 min. Finally, the mixture was poured into a 25 mL teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 24 h in an oven. Subsequently the subsidence was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with de-ionized water and absolute alcohol, then dried at 60 °C.
BiOI nanosheets materials was prepared according to our previous report [37].

The crystalline structure of the samples was determined by power X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Shimadzu XRD-6000 X-ray diffractometer using Cu-Ka radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). The UV–vis diffuse refection spectra (DRS) were recorded on an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450 Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) using BaSO4 powder as the substrate. The morphology and structure were tested using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7001F, JEOL) equipped with an energy-dispersive and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on a Nicolet Model Nexus 470 FT-IR equipment using KBr pellet technique. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were performed on a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer with the spin-trap reagent DMPO (Sigma Chemical Co.) in methanol and water.

Photodegradation experiments of the synthetic samples were performed in a Pyrex photocatalytic reactor equipped with a 300 W Xe lamp with a 400 nm cutoff filter as the lamp-house. The photocatalytic reactions were carried out under the following conditions: 100 mL organic pollutant RhB aqueous solution (10 mg L−1) mixed with 20 mg photocatalysts was placed in a container with continuous stirred. Similarly, 100 mL colorless CIP aqueous solution (10 mg L−1) mixed with 50 mg photocatalysts was placed in a container with continuously stirred. Previous to irradiation, the mixed solution was stirred for 30 min to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. In the photocatalytic degradation process, about 3 mL reaction suspension was sampled at certain time and determined by a UV–vis spectro-photometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu), according to its absorbance wavelength of 553 nm for RhB and 276 nm for CIP.

The photoelectrochemical analysis is widely known as a method to investigate the transfer of photo-induced carriers of the photocatalysts. In this system, an electrochemical analyzer with a standard three-electrode system was taken to test the photoelectrochemical properties of as-prepared materials, in which a platinum wire as counter electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode and the ITO glass (0.5 × 1 cm2) covered with Bi4O5I2 sample (0.1 mg) as working electrode, respectively. A 500 W xenon lamp was used as the photosource. The photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement were performed in a 0.1 M phosphate buffered saline solution with pH = 7 and a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)) 6 4−, respectively.

Fig. 1 illustrates the crystal structure of as-synthesized samples. The diffraction peaks of the BiOI sample were readily indexed to the tetragonal phase of BiOI (JCPDS card no. 10–0445). With respect to the Bi4O5I2 sample, it can be observed that the characteristic peaks located at 28.8°, 31.5°, 36.9°, 42.1°, 45.1°, 49.3°, 54.4° could be indexed to (-4 -1 1), (4 0 2), (−4 0 4), (−3 2 3), (4 2 2), (0 0 6), (8 1 1) planes of Bi4O5I2 materials [34,35]. The characteristic diffraction peaks of Bi4O5I2 sample are distinctly different with the BiOI materials, which indicates the crystal phase and structure are distinct from BiOI materials.

Fig. 2 exhibits the XPS spectra of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets. The survey XPS results (Fig. 2a) demonstrate that Bi, O and I are the main elements for as-prepared Bi4O5I2 nanosheets. The carbon peak at 284.6 eV is assigned to adsorbed carbon on the surface of the product. Fig. 2b shows two peaks of the Bi region at 158.8 and 164.0 eV correspond to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively. The peak binding energy of 529.5 eV belongs to O 1s and the other two peaks around 531.1 and 532.5 eV are ascribed to the chemisorbed oxygen and surface oxygen by hydroxyl species, respectively (Fig. 2c) [41]. Meanwhile, the two peaks situated at 618.9 and 630.3 eV were attributed to the I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 (Fig. 2d), respectively.

Fig. 3 shows the morphologies of the as-synthesized Bi4O5I2 and BiOI samples. From the SEM images, ultrasmall nanosheets structure can be observed and it can be noticed that these nanosheets are comprised of the size about tens of nanometers in length and a few nanometers in width (Fig. 3a). In order to further investigate the Bi4O5I2 structure, TEM analysis was performed. In Fig. 3b, it can be seen a great quantity of ultrasmall nanosheets, whose thickness was about 5–6 nm. Fig. S1 was the diameter distribution of the as-prepared Bi4O5I2 nanomaterials. As can be seen, the Bi4O5I2 diameter is mainly distributed in the range of 37–52 nm. From the HRTEM (Fig. 3c), it can be found that it was about 6 layers for the Bi4O5I2 ultrasmall nanosheets, revealing the few-layer structure. The SEM and TEM analysis indicate the ultrasmall few-layer Bi4O5I2 nanosheets have been prepared successfully. Fig. 3d displays the SEM image of the as-prepared BiOI materials. It can be seen that BiOI sample is nanosheet structure and the dimensions are in hundreds of nanometers, which is larger than Bi4O5I2 ultrasmall nanosheets. Neat smaller size guarantee the Bi4O5I2 nanosheets own higher specific surface area and provide more exposed active sites for the photcatalytic reaction.

Fig. 4 exhibits the UV–vis DRS patterns of as-prepared BiOI nanosheets and Bi4O5I2 nanosheets, which provides important evidence of their photocatalytic activity [38]. Apparently, both the BiOI and Bi4O5I2 samples show strong photoabsorption from ultraviolet to visible light region. From Fig. 4, the absorption edge of BiOI nanosheets is about 650 nm, while the absorption edge for Bi4O5I2 nanosheets is 510 nm, which shows a blue shift to BiOI. The BiOI displays the enhanced photoabsorption from 430 nm to 650 nm and the Bi4O5I2 nanosheets have higher photoabsorption from 200 nm to 430 nm. By the plot of (αhν)1/2 vs. photo energy (hν), the band gap energies for BiOI nanosheets and Bi4O5I2 nanosheets were calculated to be 1.77 and 2.18 eV, respectively (Fig. 4b).

The specific surface area and porosity of Bi4O5I2 sample were investigated by means of nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms. Fig. 5 reveals the isotherm of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets. The BET surface area of the BiOI nanosheets (calculated to be 5.57 m2 g−1) was much smaller than the prepared Bi4O5I2 sample that was calculated to be 49.04 m2 g−1, which was in accord with SEM and TEM analyses. The specific surface area of Bi4O5I2 sample is much larger than pure BiOI which can be attributed to the ultrasmall nanostructure of Bi4O5I2 material, and formation of pores in the process of the accumulation of Bi4O5I2 particles. As is known to all, the larger specific surface areas can favor the adsorption of more active sites and substrate on the surface, which may promote photocatalytic activity under the visible irradiation. Therefore, it can be forecasted the Bi4O5I2 nanosheets with lager specific surface may own the improved degradation ability.

The Raman spectrum of as-prepared materials is shown as Fig. 6 . Compared with BiOI nanosheets, the red shift of two conspicuous vibrational peaks at 106 and 151 cm−1 and decrease of peak intensity can be attributed to the change of crystal structure, and the weak Raman band at 64.7 cm−1 is caused by Bi O I stretching mode [39], which indicates that the molecular structure of as-prepared catalytic agent is different from BiOI nanosheets [40].

Generally speaking, higher photocurrent response of the photocatalyst means higher separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and thus may bring higher photocatalytic activity during photodegradation process [42]. Photocurrent response curves of the BiOI and Bi4O5I2 nanosheet samples are exhibited in Fig. 7 a. Bi4O5I2 nanosheet displays the higher photocurrent intensity than the BiOI which indicates that the Bi4O5I2 nanosheets structure facilitated the electron-hole separation. What's more, the EIS measurements are performed to further investigate the resistance of interfacial charge-transfer process. As is shown in Fig. 7b, the diameter of Nyquist circle of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets is smaller than that of the BiOI. The smaller diameter means a lower resistance of electron transfer and may enable higher separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs [43]. The stronger photocurrent intensity and the smaller resistance of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets demonstrate that the Bi4O5I2 nanosheets structure can improve the separation and transfer efficiency of charge carriers and thus enhance the photocatalytic activity. Photolumiescence (PL) technique is further performed to confirm the transfer and recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes for the BiOI and Bi4O5I2 nanomaterials during the process of photocatalysis. As shown in Fig. S2, the Bi4O5I2 nanosheets exhibit much lower intensity than the BiOI sample, which indicated that the Bi4O5I2 nanosheets possess lower recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carriers. The results are consistent with the photocurrent and EIS analysis.

To evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the synthesized Bi4O5I2 nanosheets samples, RhB was chosen as the target pollutant under the visible light irradiation. For comparion, the degradation performance of TiO2, C3N4 and BiOI nanosheets were also researched. Fig. 8 a exhibits that Bi4O5I2 nanosheets can only absorb a slight amount of RhB without visible light irradiation. As can be seen from Fig. 8b, as-prepared Bi4O5I2 nanosheets shows higher photocatalytic activity than Degussa P25 TiO2 and graphite-like carbon nitride (C3N4). At the same time, the degradation efficiency of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets is also higher than BiOI nanosheets. After irradiation for 120 min under visible light, only 4%, 9% and 50% of RhB can be removed by C3N4, TiO2 and BiOI nanosheets, while the degradation efficiency of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets could achieve 99% for the same irradiation time. As-prepared Bi4O5I2 nanosheets show the excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB under visible light. Fig. 8c shows the photocatalytic degradation kinetics of RhB over different photocatalysts, the experimental data are fitted by a pseudo-first-order kinetic model. The ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets sample possesses the maximum rate constant of 0.0319 min−1, which was almost 2.7 times higher than BiOI nanosheets.

Ciprofloxacin (CIP), widely used in medicine owing to its excellent sterilization capability, was used to further verify the degradation ability of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets. From Fig. 9 , almost no removal of CIP can be seen by C3N4 and BiOI nanosheets under visible light illumination, while Bi4O5I2 nanosheets can reach 80% nearly for CIP removal after 2 h. It is in good accord with the characterizations previously mentioned.

To evaluate the reusability and stability of ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets material, recycling reactions were performed by degrading RhB under visible light irradiation. Fig. 10 displays that no apparent deactivation of the photocatalysts is observed after five successive cycles. The results imply the high stability of ultrasmall Bi4O5I2 nanosheets. Furthermore, the XRD spectrum was applied to investigate the crystal structure of the Bi4O5I2 sample after photocatalytic cycle experiment. As shown in Fig. 11 , no obviously changes could be observed, which implies the Bi4O5I2 nanosheets materials are stable in the photocatalytic degradation procedure.

To further determine the main active species during the degradation of pollutant, a radicals trapping experiment was taken and the results were shown in Fig. 12 . The scavengers included triethanolamine for h+, benzoquinone for O2
−, isopropanol and tert-butyl alcohol for OH were used to confirm the active species during the degradation experiment [37,44]. After adding triethanolamine, the inhibitory photocatalytic activity was observed apparently, while it could be observed the photocatalytic activity was not obviously inhibited after trapping O2
− with adding benzoquinon. Therefore, it can be preliminarily ascertained that the superoxide radical does not play a major role while hole plays the main role in RhB photodegradation. In addition, the degradation efficiency declined a little when isopropanol or tert-butyl alcohol were added, implying that ·OH also participate in the photocatalytic process. Since the h+ is always the main active specie for BiOI and ·OH can not be generated for its not enough positive VB position in generally, the generated ·OH in Bi4O5I2 can lead to the increased photocatalytic activity for the pollutant degradation.

To further confirm the reactive oxygen species produced in the photocatalytic process, the electron spin resonance spin-trap technique was employed in this work. In the ESR spin-trap tests, O2
− radicals can be trapped by DMPO in the methanol dispersion [45]. As shown in Fig. 13 a, the characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2
− cannot be detected under visible light irradiation, which is consistent with the result of benzoquinone for O2
− trapping experiment. Similarly, $OH radicals can be trapped by DMPO in the aqueous dispersion. The characteristic peaks of DMPO-$OH (Fig. 13b) can be observed under visible light irradiation, which indicates that the reactive oxygen species $OH could be generated in this system. The results are consistent with the trapping experiment.

The band-edge positions of the bismuth oxyiodide were reckoned by E VB = X-E e+0.5E g, and the conduction band potential can be determined by E CB = E VB- E g. The X is the electronegativity of the corresponding semiconductors and the values for BiOI and Bi4O5I2 are ca. 5.94 and 5.93 eV, respectively. Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (4.5 eV), and Eg is the band gap energy of the photocatalyst. According to the DRS analysis, the Eg of BiOI and Bi4O5I2 was determined to be 1.77 and 2.18 eV, respectively. Thus the E VB of BiOI and Bi4O5I2 is estimated to be 2.33 eV and 2.52 eV, and the E CB of BiOI and Bi4O5I2 is 0.56 eV and 0.34 eV, respectively. Considering the E0(·OH/OH−) (2.38 eV vs NHE) and E0(O2/O2 $−) (−0.046 eV vs NHE) [40], the VB (2.52 eV) potential of Bi4O5I2 is able to oxidize OH− to yield ·OH and the CB (0.34 eV) potential is too low to reduce O2 to generate O2 $− (Fig. 14 ). Combined with the trapping experiment, ESR analysis and the band structure position (ECB = 0.34 eV), it could be assumed that the O2 $− may be not the main active species during the photoreaction process. The lowered VB position enable the generation of ·OH and thus the higher photocatalytic activity [46]. Morever, the enhanced VB width of Bi4O5I2 than BiOI favors the inhibiting of the recombination of electron-hole pairs.

Ultrasmall few-layer Bi4O5I2 nanosheets photocatalysts were successfully prepared using ILs-mannitol-assisted solvothermal method by adjusting the pH to 12. SEM, TEM and DLS analysis exhibited the ultrasmall few-layer structure of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets and the PL analysis showed the Bi4O5I2 owned the lower recombination rate for electron-hole pairs. The bismuth-rich strategy of the Bi4O5I2 enable the lower of VB position for Bi4O5I2 when compared to BiOI. RhB and CIP were used as target pollutants to estimate the photocatalytic performance of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets. The h+ and ·OH were determined to be the main active species for Bi4O5I2 during the photodegradation process rather than the h+ for BiOI by trapping experiment and ESR analysis. The ultrasmall few-layer structure and wider VB of Bi4O5I2 favor the higher separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs, which endow Bi4O5I2 with superior activity than BiOI.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 21576123, 21471069 and 21476098) and the Science and Technology support program of Zhenjiang (SH2014018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2016.10.203.
